analysis_summary: The proposed method produced 11 finding(s) from 12 analysis obligation(s).

findings.summary: External/simulation debug request paths reach dm_top debug controls without a visible top-level authorization gate using jtag_unlock or debug_mode_i.
findings.vulnerability_category: trigger debug entry/reset and issue DMI/debug requests / debug capability, ndmreset_o, dmactive_o, debug_req_o, dm_slave/dm_master paths / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: external JTAG/DTM or simulation debug requester via tck_i/tms_i/td_i/trst_ni, SimDTM, SimJTAG, or dmi_jtag Operation: trigger debug entry/reset and issue DMI/debug requests Object: debug capability, ndmreset_o, dmactive_o, debug_req_o, dm_slave/dm_master paths Expected guard: debug authorization/authentication such as jtag_unlock, lifecycle/test-mode restriction, or debug_mode_i mediation before debug requests/reset/bus access Observed behavior: dmi_jtag produces jtag_unlock and DMI requests, but the visible top-level path wires debug_req/debug_req_valid into dm_top; jtag_unlock is not shown as a guard at dm_top. SimDTM similarly drives debug request signals in simulation. Debug master system-bus grant/response are tied off, but a debug NoC AXI-lite bridge is wired to dm_master AXI signals. Path: JTAG pins/SimDTM -> dmi_jtag/SimDTM -> debug_req/debug_req_valid -> dm_top -> ndmreset_o/dmactive_o/debug_req_o; debug NoC bridge -> dm_master AXI-lite signals Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: If dmi_jtag/SimDTM internals do not enforce authentication before DMI request generation, debug entry/reset may be requested by an external/simulation requester.

findings.summary: reglk_wrapper treats jtag_unlock as a reset-like clear of all lock registers, bypassing sticky lock policy if jtag_unlock is asserted.
findings.vulnerability_category: clear/override register-lock policy / reglk_ctrl lock bits controlling peripheral write protection / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: JTAG/debug unlock path and AXI register writer Operation: clear/override register-lock policy Object: reglk_ctrl lock bits controlling peripheral write protection Expected guard: jtag_unlock authenticated and unable to clear/bypass sticky locks except under permitted lifecycle/debug authorization Observed behavior: reglk_wrapper reset condition is ~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9). When jtag_unlock is high, reglk_mem entries are set to zero. No additional guard is visible inside reglk_wrapper besides raw jtag_unlock. jtag_unlock is sourced from dmi_jtag. Path: dmi_jtag.jtag_unlock_o -> top-level jtag_unlock -> reglk_wrapper.jtag_unlock -> reset branch clearing reglk_mem -> reglk_ctrl_o -> peripheral reglk_ctrl_i slices Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: JTAG unlock can erase all register locks; if jtag_unlock is not strongly authenticated, lock protections are bypassable.

findings.summary: MMIO/NoC writes can modify and clear reglk lock state whenever the access-control bit is enabled and selected incoming lock bits do not block writes; locks are not set-only sticky.
findings.vulnerability_category: write or clear lock-control registers / software-visible register-lock control state reglk_mem/reglk_ctrl_o / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: AXI/MMIO requester reaching reglk_wrapper through NoC AXI-lite bridge Operation: write or clear lock-control registers Object: software-visible register-lock control state reglk_mem/reglk_ctrl_o Expected guard: writes restricted by existing lock bits, privilege/access-control policy, and sticky semantics preventing unauthorized clearing Observed behavior: reglk_wrapper enable is en_acct && acct_ctrl_i with acct_ctrl_i from acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][9]. Writes update reglk_mem with wdata on en && we and selected reglk_ctrl_i bit clear. No requester identity/privilege attribute is propagated through AXI-lite beyond top-level priv_lvl_i. Writable entries can be overwritten with arbitrary wdata including zero. reset/rst_9/jtag_unlock also clear locks to zero. Path: buf_ariane_reglk_noc2_* -> noc_axilite_bridge -> reglk_axi_req -> axi_lite_interface -> address/en/we/wdata -> reglk_mem -> reglk_ctrl_o Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: A current-privilege NoC/MMIO requester with acct bit enabled can program or clear lock policy; lock state lacks sticky set-only semantics while unlocked.

findings.summary: Access-control policy registers are writable from the ACCT NoC/MMIO path when acc_ctrl_c for the current priv_lvl permits access; default reset permits all policy bits and external we_flag can force part of policy permissive.
findings.vulnerability_category: configure/change peripheral access policy / acc_ctrl access-control matrix for peripherals / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: AXI/MMIO requester or CPU privilege mode Operation: configure/change peripheral access policy Object: acc_ctrl access-control matrix for peripherals Expected guard: policy registers writable only by authorized privileged software and protected by reglk_ctrl_i/acct_ctrl_i; unauthorized privilege levels cannot change policy Observed behavior: acct_wrapper is reached through a NoC AXI-lite bridge. en is en_acct && acct_ctrl_i, with acct_ctrl_i wired to acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][6]. On reset/rst_6, acct_mem is initialized to 32'hffffffff. Writes on en && we update acct_mem entries unless selected reglk_ctrl bits block them. acc_ctrl_o includes acct_mem[0] OR {8{we_flag}}, so we_flag can force lower 8 access-control bits high. Path: buf_ariane_acct_noc2_* -> acct noc_axilite_bridge -> acct_axi_req -> acct_wrapper address/en/we/wdata -> acct_mem -> acc_ctrl_o -> acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][peripheral] gates Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Policy state may be changed by any requester matching the current access-control bit, and reset defaults fail open; unauthorized or low-privilege modification cannot be excluded from visible evidence.

findings.summary: acc_ctrl_c intentionally or accidentally aliases peripheral index 5 to index 4 via OR, granting j==5 access when j==4 bit is set even if j==5 bit is clear.
findings.vulnerability_category: route/authorize peripheral read/write requests / peripheral access decisions derived from acc_ctrl_c / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: CPU/bus requester evaluated by priv_lvl_i Operation: route/authorize peripheral read/write requests Object: peripheral access decisions derived from acc_ctrl_c Expected guard: each protected peripheral request gated by correct per-privilege/peripheral access-control bit, with no unintended alias/OR granting access across peripherals Observed behavior: For every privilege i and peripheral j, acc_ctrl_c[i][j] = acc_ctrl[j*4+i] | (j==5 && acc_ctrl[4*4+i]). Thus for j==5, acc_ctrl_c includes both its own bit and peripheral 4's bit. Reset defaults acc_ctrl to all ones via acct_wrapper. Top-level uses acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][index] for wrapper/peripheral access controls (e.g. ROM index 0, HMAC index 4, ACCT index 6, REGLK index 9, RST index 12, RNG index 10). Path: acc_ctrl -> acc_ctrl_c[i][j] assignment -> peripheral acct_ctrl_i/request gates such as wrapper acct_ctrl_i and rom_req Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Peripheral index 5 receives access authorization from peripheral index 4, potentially granting access across peripheral policy domains.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Peripheral mapping for j==5 and all j==5 use sites are not fully present in the provided facts.

findings.summary: Top-level crypto NoC paths are mediated by wrapper acct_ctrl_i/reglk_ctrl_i/debug_mode_i ports, but wrapper internals and response-data exposure are not available; no closure on secret-state readback.
findings.vulnerability_category: read, write, configure, or expose crypto/RNG state over NoC/AXI / crypto accelerator registers, keys, random data, intermediate state, and response data paths / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: NoC/AXI requester to crypto peripheral ports Operation: read, write, configure, or expose crypto/RNG state over NoC/AXI Object: crypto accelerator registers, keys, random data, intermediate state, and response data paths Expected guard: crypto/key/status registers and output data mediated by acc_ctrl/reglk/privilege guards and not exposing secret state via read muxes/stale outputs Observed behavior: Top-level instantiates crypto wrappers such as RNG, HMAC, AES, RSA, SHA and connects each to a noc_axilite_bridge. Visible wrapper ports include reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i(acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][index]), debug_mode_i, AXI req/resp, and reset outputs. However actual wrapper read/write gating and response mux behavior are not included. Path: crypto NoC ports -> noc_axilite_bridge -> crypto wrapper AXI req/resp -> NoC response outputs Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: Cannot determine whether keys/RNG/intermediate state are protected from readback or stale exposure; top-level shows intended guard inputs but not sufficient use-site evidence.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Crypto wrapper internals, address decode, read muxes, and secret register exposure are absent.

findings.summary: NoC/MMIO writes to rst_wrapper can set reset outputs for security-sensitive peripherals when acct_ctrl_i permits; reset command path lacks visible reglk gating at the command write site and debug_mode_i behavior is uncertain.
findings.vulnerability_category: trigger or configure reset of protected peripherals / peripheral reset control outputs and reset memory/state rst_mem / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: AXI/MMIO requester and debug-mode requester Operation: trigger or configure reset of protected peripherals Object: peripheral reset control outputs and reset memory/state rst_mem Expected guard: software-triggered resets limited by privilege/access-control and reglk policy; debug_mode_i exceptions explicitly authorized Observed behavior: rst_wrapper is reached via RST NoC AXI-lite bridge. rst outputs are direct assignments from rst_mem bits. When start is asserted, case(rst_id) sets rst_mem bit for ROM, AES, SHA, HMAC, PKT, ACCT, DMA, REGLK, RNG, AES2, RSA, and reset controller. en is en_acct && acct_ctrl_i. Provided truncated evidence does not show use of reglk_ctrl_i/debug_mode_i in command update logic. Path: buf_ariane_rst_noc2_* -> rst noc_axilite_bridge -> rst_axi_req -> rst_wrapper address/en/we/wdata/start/rst_id -> rst_mem -> rst_1..rst_13 outputs Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: A permitted MMIO requester can reset protected crypto, ACCT, and REGLK blocks; if reset access-control is permissive or debug_mode_i bypasses checks, unauthorized reset of security state may be possible.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Truncated file omitted start/rst_id update code and whether reglk_ctrl_i/debug_mode_i gate writes.

findings.summary: Software-controlled reset outputs rst_6 and rst_9 can reset ACCT and REGLK policy state; their reset defaults are permissive/open (acct all ones, reglk all zeros).
findings.vulnerability_category: reset/clear permission policy or lock state / access-control registers acc_ctrl and lock registers reglk_ctrl / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: reset controller requester through AXI/MMIO/debug Operation: reset/clear permission policy or lock state Object: access-control registers acc_ctrl and lock registers reglk_ctrl Expected guard: reset controls must not clear lock/access policy state unless authorized, and reset defaults should fail closed or preserve protections Observed behavior: rst_wrapper maps rst_id 6 to ACCT reset rst_6 and rst_id 9 to REGLK reset rst_9. acct_wrapper reset condition ~(rst_ni && ~rst_6) initializes all acct_mem entries to 32'hffffffff. reglk_wrapper reset condition including rst_9 initializes all reglk_mem entries to zero. Thus local resets erase policy/locks to permissive/unlocked defaults. Path: RST NoC/MMIO command -> rst_wrapper start/rst_id -> rst_mem[6]/rst_mem[9] -> acct_wrapper.rst_6 / reglk_wrapper.rst_9 -> acct_mem/reglk_mem defaults -> acc_ctrl/reglk_ctrl Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: A reset command can erase access-control and lock state to open/unlocked values, enabling subsequent access or policy modification if reset command path is reachable.

findings.summary: Fuse memory output supplies JTAG authentication hashes and fuse_req/fuse_addr are visible, but no privilege/access-control/reglk/debug guard is shown on the fuse read interface in the provided top-level evidence.
findings.vulnerability_category: read or expose fuse data / fuse values/fuse_rdata and hash outputs / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: bus/NoC/debug requester to fuse interface Operation: read or expose fuse data Object: fuse values/fuse_rdata and hash outputs Expected guard: fuse reads mediated by privilege/access-control, lifecycle/debug authorization, and lock policy; fuse_rdata not exposed on unprotected paths Observed behavior: fuse_mem instance outputs jtag_hash, okey_hash, ikey_hash to top-level signals used by dmi_jtag. It accepts req_i fuse_req, addr_i fuse_addr, and outputs fuse_rdata. Provided evidence does not identify guard conditions on fuse_req/fuse_addr nor a protected readback mux for fuse_rdata. Path: fuse_req/fuse_addr -> fuse_mem -> fuse_rdata and jtag_hash/okey_hash/ikey_hash -> dmi_jtag hash inputs Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Potential unguarded fuse read/exposure path; particularly sensitive because fuse outputs feed debug authentication material.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Source of fuse_req/fuse_addr and destination/readback path of fuse_rdata are not provided.

findings.summary: debug_mode_i is propagated into rst_wrapper and crypto wrappers, but provided evidence does not include the conditional branches using it; cannot determine whether it bypasses permission checks.
findings.vulnerability_category: override, reset, or change security policy in debug mode / permission checks and reset controls affected by debug mode / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: debug-mode state input Operation: override, reset, or change security policy in debug mode Object: permission checks and reset controls affected by debug mode Expected guard: debug_mode_i should not by itself authorize protected resets, lock clearing, or policy modification unless paired with authenticated debug state Observed behavior: Top-level debug_mode_i port is wired to rst_wrapper and crypto wrappers. rst_wrapper declares debug_mode_i, but the visible portion only shows reset vector logic, AXI-lite connection, and en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i; truncated command decode may contain debug_mode_i behavior. No relationship to jtag_unlock is visible. Path: debug_mode_i -> rst_wrapper and crypto wrapper ports -> potential permission/reset behavior changes Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: Cannot close whether debug_mode_i is safe or a bypass. If used alone to enable writes/resets, it could authorize protected operations without authenticated debug.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: All uses/branches involving debug_mode_i inside rst_wrapper and crypto wrappers were not fully observed.

findings.summary: External we_flag_0 directly forces part of acc_ctrl_o high independent of AXI writes and lock/access checks; we_flag_4 feeds dmi_jtag and may affect debug authentication behavior, but internals are unavailable.
findings.vulnerability_category: enable writes/configuration changes / security-relevant register update enables and access-control outputs / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: external write-enable flag source Operation: enable writes/configuration changes Object: security-relevant register update enables and access-control outputs Expected guard: external write-enable flags affecting security registers generated by authorized logic and combined with lock/access checks at update site Observed behavior: acct_wrapper acc_ctrl_o ORs acct_mem[0] with {8{we_flag}}, and top-level maps we_flag_0 into acct_wrapper.we_flag. This bypasses the acct_mem update path and reglk lock checks by forcing lower 8 access-control outputs high whenever we_flag_0 is asserted. Top-level maps we_flag_4 into dmi_jtag.we_flag; no internals show guard. Other top-level we_flag_1..3 uses are not shown in provided excerpts. Path: we_flag_0 top-level input -> acct_wrapper.we_flag -> acc_ctrl_o lower 8 bits -> acc_ctrl_c gates; we_flag_4 -> dmi_jtag.we_flag Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: An external signal can independently force access-control permissions open for affected bits; if source is untrusted, this bypasses policy register locks and MMIO authorization.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Uses of we_flag_1..we_flag_3 and internal dmi_jtag we_flag semantics are not provided.

global_uncertainty: No closure-level missing evidence was recorded beyond the visible source scope limitations.
